g.data-narration: Biomedical Waste Management is crucial for healthcare safety and environmental protection according to Indian Rules 2016.
Biomedical waste includes all waste from healthcare facilities during diagnosis, treatment, or research activities.
g.text: Any waste generated during diagnosis, treatment, immunization of humans/animals,
or in research activities. Governed by Biomedical Waste Management Rules, 2016 (India).

g.data-narration: Only 15% of healthcare waste is hazardous, but improper management causes infections, injuries, and environmental pollution.
g.text: • Improper handling causes infections, needlestick injuries, environmental pollution
• WHO estimates 15% of healthcare waste poses serious health risks

g.data-narration: Biomedical waste is classified into 9 categories under Indian Rules. Each category requires specific handling.
g.text: Color Coding: Yellow (anatomical/soiled), Red (microbiological), Blue/White (sharps), Black (chemical)

g.data-narration: Categories 1-3: Human anatomical waste includes tissues and organs. Animal waste from veterinary sources. Microbiological waste contains cultures and vaccines.
g.text: Veterinary & research animal tissues, carcasses. Incineration ≥1200°C.
Cultures, vaccines, specimens. Autoclaving or incineration.

g.data-narration: Categories 4-6: Sharps are dangerous causing needlestick injuries. Discarded medicines include cytotoxic drugs. Solid waste includes soiled dressings and gloves.
g.text: Expired drugs, cytotoxic drugs. Incineration or secured landfill.
Soiled dressings, gloves, catheters, blood bags. Autoclaving.

g.data-narration: Categories 7-9: Liquid waste includes blood and body fluids. Incineration ash contains hazardous residues. Chemical waste includes disinfectants and solvents.
g.text: Ash from incineration. May contain hazardous residues. Secured landfill.
Formaldehyde, glutaraldehyde, solvents. Appropriate treatment or incineration.

g.data-narration: Physical characteristics include mixed composition (plastics 40-50%), moisture content 30-80%, and calorific value 2000-4500 kcal/kg.
g.text: Moisture: 30-80% | Calorific Value: 2,000-4,500 kcal/kg | Density: 150-400 kg/m³

g.data-narration: Chemical characteristics include high organic content 40-60%, hazardous chemicals like formaldehyde, and chlorine content 1-3%.
Biological characteristics include pathogenic microorganisms like bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites with variable pathogen loads.
Biomedical waste has five hazardous properties: infectious, toxic, corrosive, flammable, and reactive.
Treatment methods include incineration (1200°C), autoclaving, microwave treatment, and chemical disinfection based on waste category.
Learn the waste generation formula: Daily waste = beds × kg/bed/day. Monthly = daily × 30 days. Infectious = total × percentage.
g.text: Daily Waste = Beds × Waste per bed per day

g.data-narration: Key compliance: segregation at source reduces costs 70-80%. Color coding mandatory. Documentation and annual returns required.
This table summarizes all 9 waste categories with their examples and treatment methods for quick reference.
Remember: Proper biomedical waste management protects health workers, patients, and the environment. Follow the Rules 2016!
g.text: ✓ Know the 9 Categories | ✓ Proper Segregation | ✓ Appropriate Treatment | ✓ Follow Rules 2016
Biomedical Waste Management protects healthcare workers, patients, and the environment!
